现金的消亡与货币的未来之战。在一个未来中，我们有一个私人的，匿名的现金替代品，但在“黑镜”中，您口袋里的钱就知道关于您的一切。
  随着现金的流失，隐私面临着极为不确定的未来。在我关于隐私硬币的文章中，我说过政府将在二十年内驱使实物现金灭绝。现在，我觉得它甚至不需要那么长时间。
 中国不懈地努力建设国家支持的数字货币，全球大流行席卷全球的经济灾难，以及像比特币和以太坊这样的分散式加密货币的惊人崛起，加快了兑现现金的时间表。
  它是像Monero这样的去中心化，无边界，开源，保护隐私的资金吗？还是由国家推动的数字监控资金？
   开源社区从强大的社区开始，在集中力量了解它们受到打击之前，发布了比特币和基于隐私的替代方案，例如Zcash和Monero。但是，各个国家/地区的州都在密切关注，他们已经学会并快速适应了。世界上几乎每个中央银行都在迅速制定自己的数字货币计划。
  一种是现金被消灭，民族数字货币是货币的主要形式。这笔钱与您的集中身份和位置数据有关，您所做的一切都由功能强大的政府计算机记录下来，这些计算机对您的了解比对您的了解要多。严格的法律也禁止和破坏分散的货币。
 第二种情况是民族国家的钱砸了钱，但我们看到了与该民族国家的钱同时发展的开放，隐私保护数字货币的并行经济操作系统。公开货币反映了现金的相同匿名属性。 
我认为，除了在黑市中，仍有一些现金被凑整和烧掉之后，仍然没有实物现金存在的未来。但是最终，实物商品将耗尽，并且随着旧钞票的退化，将不会有中央银行印制更多的钞票，并且它们会慢慢消失。
     毫无疑问，为金钱的未来而战是为控制而战。
  每当我们在社会上做事的结构发生变化时，力量平衡就会随之变化。当控制权易手时，有人会获得更多权力，有人会失去权力。
 我们已经看到这种钟摆在整个历史的弧度中来回摇摆。
   在西方，在希腊人时代，分散的“城市国家”的分散力量被罗马帝国的中央集权和威严所取代。但是随后帝国崩溃了，取而代之的是一些由基督教徒束缚而又在希腊的艺术，建筑和数学理想中幸存下来的，正在转变的民族国家。在整个人类历史中，权力下放到集中并再次返回。
 金钱的演变反映了人类历史的进程。随着人们希望找到一种替代物物交换的方式，它随着时间的推移有机地进化。金钱变成了诺言。我们都同意它的价值X，我们可以用它来交换从马到奶酪的任何东西。
 在国家不稳定的时候，金钱更多地是本地的。它的变化速度通常与因剑而死的统治者一样快。他们会征服领土，但几年后却失去了领土。 
但是随着人们开始与人们进行交易，他们并不知道他们需要更多的通用货币。为什么在蒙古国广阔草原或北非丘陵地区的某人会接受印有当地统治者面孔的硬币，而这些统治者对他们无能为力？因此，通用货币得以发展，国际交易者随后可以兑换回本地货币。
 在丝绸之路上，国际贸易商为丝绸提供了其他任何商品或服务，因为丝绸非常有价值。贸易商知道他们可以轻松地以非常稳定的价格将其卖回国内。丝绸是最早的真正国际货币之一，它为国家，部落和王国之间的贸易提供了前所未有的便利。在其他时候，不同的商品可以充当金钱的代名词，例如胡椒，盐和树皮碎片。盐是如此强大，以至于是一种货币，以至于“薪水”一词来自给予罗马士兵的盐度口粮，即所谓的“阿根廷腊肠”。
 正是这些广受喜爱的商品充当了价值的存储，迅速取代了物物交换，这是一件好事。易货交易是一种效率极低的系统。这使得每个人都很难在需要时得到他们想要的东西。
 也许如果您想用奶酪换面包，那就不难了。两种商品都不会占用很多空间。双方都只是将奶酪或面包放在挎包中进行交换。
 但是，如果您马匹丰富，并且想为三匹马买六个炉子怎么办？
 现在，熔炉制造商还必须拥有养马的方法，因此他也从事稳定的业务。如果他不想要你的马，那你就买不到炉子。由于您拥有丰富的马匹，因此别无他物。同时，镇上的其他人迫切希望马带家人去另一个镇，但他只有铁铲，养马者不需要这些东西。
 象征性的金钱使一切变得容易。这家伙直接将马卖给想搬家的家庭。然后，饲养员拿走了他的钱，买了炉子。炉子人可以买任何他想要的东西，而他不必担心马的照顾和喂养。 
民族国家的崛起反映了货币的崛起。随着小部族逐渐沦为封建领地，突袭和战争将边界推到更稳定的状态，金钱演变成更普遍接受的标准。我们越来越多地看到诸如金和银之类的贵金属在人们所认为的货币中占主导地位。然后将珍贵的奖章压入具有固定价值的硬币中。装完硬币后，我们开始使用由贵金属商店支持的小纸片。最终，随着各国脱离金本位制，我们最终切断了与贵金属的联系。
   就像我们看到化学胶片在一百多年的发展过程中，变得越来越精致一样，只是突然被数码相机遮盖住了，纸币也将遭受同样的命运。它会被流动性强，易于转移的数字货币所取代。
  向无现金社会的转变始于多年前。中国已经是一个基本上没有现金的社会，与我在2014年访问时才7年前的巨大而迅速的变化相比，现金仍然是王者。仅仅七年后，中国86％的人使用移动支付，跳过了古老的Visa和Mastercard PoS系统，转而使用无处不在的QR码。
 2016年底，总理纳伦达·莫迪（Narenda Modi）突然用笔轻便禁止了该国大部分货币。它本应减少腐败并让人们纳税。他们一年后回溯了，但这仅仅是因为他们移动得太快了。澳大利亚试图禁止使用现金购买1万或更多的物品。这项措施失败了，但是这些早期的失败并不意味着赢得战争，而只是战斗。
 这些想法不会消失，它们清楚地表明了政府如何看待现金：
   因为现金几乎无法控制国家。他们无法轻易看到它的去向，谁拥有它以及谁在它上面花了钱。
 民族国家已经非常习惯了这种控制。如今，地球上几乎每个人都生活在一个拥有清晰边界的民族国家，带有特殊颜色和标志（被称为国旗）的徽章以及爱国歌曲的力量之下。随着民族国家成为地球上的终极力量，他们夺取了印钞权的控制权，并消除了任何其他人利用法律和武力创造货币的权力。 
最初，印有该民族国家象征的实物现金是声望的最终来源。拥有自己的钱意味着您是成熟的独立力量。有形的金钱是一个民族的骄傲。
  上图：旧的Diner's Club卡-与我无关（我不认为）
 但这随着金钱变得越来越虚拟化而开始改变。随着虚拟化程度的提高，它变得更易于跟踪。它失去了匿名性。随着1950年代和1960年代信用卡的兴起，货币及其流动的地方变得越来越透明，由Diner’s Club和Visa等私人公司持有的实物分类账详细显示了这一动作。随着计算机和网络在1980年代和1990年代席卷全球，所有这些分类帐都变得数字化，并且变得更加透明。政府凝视着这些账本，控制这笔钱的流动变得比印钱本身更加强大。
 现在，政府习惯于查看每笔交易及其去向，他们不想失去权力。消灭现金将永远消除这种匿名性。没有人会不知道在标签销售中购买一盏灯。
 只是一个问题。他们还不能摆脱现金。
 正如我们在印度看到的那样，人们起义。他们拒绝上交账单，反正还是使用它们。一年后，印度不得不将这些票据重新投入流通，因为它们的数字系统还没有准备好处理这些负担。在澳大利亚，这种大流行病粉碎了任何幻想，即澳大利亚已经拥有足够强大的数字支付系统来代替现金，并且他们知道小型企业会采取行动，因此他们取消了停止将现金支付超过1万的计划。
 最终要杀死现金大国，国家所需要的不仅仅是法律。人们只有拥有功能强大，具有当今金钱所没有的杀手级功能的替代方案，才能接受现金的death灭。直到拥有新技术以及旧技术的几乎所有功能以及一堆新功能的人都离不开，主导技术才能消失。 
大多数民族国家对比特币及其国际化，分散化的性质感到恐惧。民族国家领导人享受货币供应的控制已久，以至于他们无法再想象像丝绸或盐之类的国际货币了。
 民族国家对加密货币感到恐惧和愤怒。当Facebook试图启动天秤座时，国会和其他世界各国政府都在追随他们。当Telegram试图构建一种可以轻松飞越加密信使的完全可扩展的数字货币时，美国政府却将其压倒了，尽管事实是它们从认可的投资者那里筹集了所有资金。
 但是，当美国和其他政府在恐惧中做出反应时，中国却密切注视着区块链，他们比世界上任何其他国家都了解自己的力量。当其他超级大国睡在区块链上，或者嘲笑比特币，或者在盲目，动物恐惧中大吃一惊时，中国开始建设。
 在所有将杀死现金的漩涡状变量中，现在是中国的数字人民币将点亮保险丝，从而使我们更快地走向全球现金爆炸性和突然死亡，而不是法律或比特币。
      他们已经在对数字人民币进行测试，进行空投，并让市民用免费的钱买东西。根据我看过的视频和评论，它是一个精巧的平台。无论用户是否处于离线状态，它都可以通过触摸手机进行交易。稍后将同步事务。
 中国很聪明。他们没有看到像Doom博士这样愚蠢的西方经济学家对比特币价格的剧烈波动的反感，而是看到了一种扭曲比特币最初的自由主义设计以建立中央控制的数字监控中心的方法。他们认为这是建立国家赞助系统的一种方法，该系统可以轻松地跨界，轻松突破国际制裁，甚至摧毁美国主导的国际银行体系。他们采用了隐私保护，去中心化的区块链基础，并将其扔到窗外，用集中控制工具取代了那些理想主义特征，这是一种基于许可的，指示性的“共识”模型，该模型使那些最初的乌托邦理想着火了。
 中国人还看到，分散的，无边界的货币受算法规则难以更改的控制，这意味着政府不再能够通过取消流通或印制更多货币来使货币贬值或膨胀。他们将失去施加货币管制的能力，或者告诉您一次可以取出多少钱或可以发送给其他人多少钱。以隐私为中心的加密货币甚至更糟，因为这意味着每个人都拥有相同的隐私，并且受到铁定的加密协议的支持，该协议不可更改，因为调查人员想采取捷径而不是真正的侦探工作。 
中国看到了一切，希望在无边无际的资金占领世界之前先行一步，因为那时一切为时已晚。因此他们行动迅速。他们禁止在中国进行比特币交易，并迫使比特币矿工进行注册。然后，他们建立了一个集中控制的数字现金版本，以迅速分散去中心化的资金。
   他们将通过“一带一路”倡议为该货币注入种子，该倡议使中国在发展中经济体上进行了大量投资，修建道路，桥梁和基础设施，或为此提供贷款。他们将很快希望发展中国家使用数字人民币偿还这些贷款。他们最初可能会遇到一些阻力，但是最终，那些没有强大的支付和银行基础设施的小国家将会看到它的简单，轻松和轻松，并且将开始越来越多地使用它。他们还将看到美国和欧洲仍在使用银行业的落后，过时和荒谬的系统，并完全跳过了这一系统。
 是否想通过西方系统汇款？写下便签，签名，扫描并传真给他人。然后，也许一天后有人去了。几天后，只要出纳员没有拧紧电线并且电线没有反弹，您的电线就会越过边界。当然，这是指第三世界国家的某人甚至可以首先获得一个银行帐户，或者如果一个小国的企业想要与美国人开展业务，甚至可以符合FACTA的所有要求。
 这样一来，他们就无法与建立有千余个构思不佳且相互冲突的扼流点的古老国际银行系统作斗争，还可以在智能手机上启动数字元钱包，或者在台式机上使用漂亮的数字仪表板，在整个过程中迅速赚钱使用中文系统以秒为单位的世界。
  当美国放弃对其他国家的领导和投资，以令人反感，自私，荒谬的“我为先”的民族民粹主义眼光转向世界时，中国人却在投资。当美国人打一场毫无意义的破坏性文化战争时，中国人正在向全球70多个不同的国家投资，这些国家的政府不稳定且基础设施不佳。他们用几条道路和桥梁购买未来，而美国则筑墙。
  当然，西方世界不想看到中华民族的货币成为非洲和远东新兴国家的主导者。世界上其他中央银行都在竞相建立自己的数字货币以应对威胁，但它们已经远远落后了。意大利希望完全没有现金。欧洲也是如此，这加强了其在数字欧元方面的工作。但是，西方大国仍在白板上制定计划，反对在中国进行实时系统的Beta测试。
 西方国家最终将迎头赶上，不久我们将进行民族国家数字货币的全面军备竞赛，所有人都在争夺霸权。其中一些将受到可怕的IT和程序员的阻碍，一些将具有糟糕的设计，一些将破坏安全性并遭到黑客入侵，但其中一些将得到正确解决，失败的国家将仅仅适应复制成功。 
有了这些新的中央银行数字货币（CBDC），您可以随时随地在您的口袋中进行全面监视。您所做的每笔交易。随处可见。所有这些都与您的身份，历史记录和地理位置数据相关。
 在那之后的十年，您将不再纳税，每次您在车库出售的二手烤面包机时，它们都会自动被淘汰。如果有错误，您会打电话要求解决，或者请一位会计师尝试为您退款，而政府会向您提供一年的免费贷款。这笔钱甚至不会从中央银行转移到私人银行机构，它将直接存入您的口袋，而那些泛黄币将使您知道从摇篮到坟墓的一切。
  尽管在加密社区中天真地宣布了相反的规定，但人们绝对会使用这些中央银行货币。甚至，他们会为此感到自豪。
  当然，只有当政府运转良好时，他们才会感到自豪和高兴。
 当事情进展顺利时，系统中的滥用行为将不会影响到足够多的人超越公众的视野。只有“坏蛋”会为此付出代价，但可能不是普通公民本人认识的任何人，因此该系统将对大多数人有效。他们不会在意隐私，因为他们只是为孩子们购买尿布，在亚马逊上购买书籍以及在当地商店购买咖啡。
 但是，当政府出现问题时（例如我们现在在美国国会大厦起义中看到的情况），他们的想法就会迅速改变。一个国家支持的货币仅与控制它的政府一样好。您必须信任它，为了信任它，您必须信任它背后的人。
  美国不是固定的东西。每个政府机构始终在运转，这是一群人。 IBM是一家公司，但是公司总是在变化。那是因为背后的人在变。除非您问哪个司法部，否则司法部不是司法部？哪年？谁在运行它？没有司法部，当时只有人在运作。 
信任取决于谁在掌权，以及他们如何使用这种权力。
  信任常常被打破。只需一次打破信任就可以永远粉碎它。一个男人可能忠于他的妻子十年，只是为了欺骗她并在一瞬间破坏所有的信任。
 “常规货币的根本问题是使货币正常运作所需的所有信任。必须信任中央银行不要贬低该货币，但法定货币的历史充满了对该信任的破坏，” Satoshi在2009年写道。
 当政府发疯时，他们现在将有权将这种发疯的情况转嫁给您。想要拿来您辛苦赚来的钱并转移到拥有更少疯狂执政者的国家吗？你不能货币控制现已到位。您的钱被冻结了。花费在国内或其他地方。
 当中国最终将其社会信用评分与本币挂钩时，他们将能够阻止某人上公交车或购买除基本食品以外的任何东西，因为他们对微信表达了党根本不喜欢的观点。
 也许您认为这是一件好事？一个民族国家不应该拥有控制资金流入和流出该国的权力吗？他们只是想保护自己的公民和经济免受危机的破坏性影响。
 但是，如果您住在冰岛，可能会有所不同，冰岛在2008年金融危机之后实行了严格的货币管制，直到10年后的2017年才实行。这意味着居住在那里的每个人只有在幻想自己从未真正拥有自己的钱时才拥有这种幻想。

The Coming Death of Cash and the Battle for the Future of Money. In One Future We have a Private, Anonymous Alternative to Cash but in the Black Mirror Future the Money in Your Pocket Knows Everything About You.
  And with the death of cash, privacy faces a deeply uncertain future. In  my article on privacy coins I said governments would drive physical cash to extinction in twenty years. Now I feel it won’t even take that long.
 China’s relentless push to build  a state backed digital currency, the economic disaster the global pandemic wreaked upon the world, and the incredible rise of decentralized cryptos like Bitcoin and Ethereum has accelerated the timeline to kill off cash.
  Will it be decentralized, borderless, open source, privacy preserving money like Monero, or will it be nation state driven digital surveillance money?
   Open source communities started off strong, releasing Bitcoin and privacy  based alternatives like Zcash and Monero before centralized powers knew  what hit them. But nations states were watching very closely and they’ve learned and adapted fast.  Almost every central bank in the world is rapidly making plans to build their own digital currencies.
  One is where cash is killed off and nation state digital money is the dominant form of money. The money is tied to your centralized identity and your location data and everything you do is recorded by powerful  government computers that understand more about you than you do about yourself. Decentralized money is also outlawed and crippled by draconian laws.
 The second scenario is where nation state money smashes cash but we see a parallel economic operating system of open, privacy preserving digital money developing alongside that nation state money. The open money mirrors the same anonymous properties of cash.
  I see no future where physical cash still exists, other than in the black market, where some of it survives being rounded up and burned. But  eventually physical goods wear out and as the old bank notes degrade  there won’t be a central bank printing more of them and they will slowly disappear.
     Make no mistake, the battle for the future of money is a battle for control.
  Whenever the structure of how we do things in society shifts, the balance of power shifts with it. Someone gains more power and someone loses power as control changes hands.
 We’ve seen this pendulum swing back and forth again and again throughout the arc of history.
   In the west, the scattered power of decentralized “ city states” in the time of the Greeks was replaced by the centralized might and majesty of the Roman Empire. But then  the empire crumbled, replaced by a collection of shifting nation states  bound loosely by Christianity and surviving Greek ideals of art, architecture and math. Decentralized to centralized and back again, over and over throughout human history.
 Money’s evolution mirrored the movement of human history. It evolved organically over time, as people wanted to find a way to replace bartering. Money became a promise. We all agree its worth X and we can trade it for anything from horses to cheese.
 When nations were unstable, money was more local. It often changed as fast as the rulers who lived and died by the sword. They would conquer territory, only to lose it a few years later.
 But as people began to trade with people they didn’t know they needed more universal kinds of money. Why would someone in the sprawling steppes of Mongolia or the hills of northern Africa accept coins stamped with the face of a local ruler who had no power over them? So universal money developed that international traders could exchange back to local money later.
 On the  silk roads, international traders paid in silk for any other good or service because silk was so valuable. Traders knew they could easily sell it  back home for a very consistent price. Silk was the one of the first true international currencies, facilitating trade between nations, tribes and kingdoms like never before in history. At other times, different goods served as a stand-in for money, like pepper,  salt, and scraps of tree bark. Salt was so powerful as a form of money that the word “salary” comes from  the salt rations given to Roman soldiers known as the “salarium argentum.”
 It was these universally loved commodities acting as a store of value that swiftly replaced bartering and that was a good thing. Bartering is a horribly inefficient system. It makes it hard for everyone to get what they want, when they want it.
 Maybe if you wanted to trade cheese for bread, that wasn’t so hard. Neither commodity takes up a lot of space. Both people just carry cheese or bread in their satchel and do the exchange.
 But what if you’re rich in horses and you want to buy six furnaces for three horses?
 Now the furnace maker also has to have a way to keep horses, so he’s in the stable business too. If he doesn’t want your horses then you can’t buy  your furnaces. Since you’re rich in horses and nothing else, you can’t  give him anything else. Meanwhile someone else in town desperately wants horses to take his family to another town but he’s only got iron  shovels and the horse breeder doesn’t need those things.
 Symbolic money made everything easier. The guy sells his horses directly to the family that wants to move. Then the horse breeder takes his money and buys furnaces. The furnace guy can buy whatever he wants and he doesn’t have to worry about the caring and feeding of horses.
 The rise of nation states mirrored the rise of money. As tiny tribes gave  way to fiefdoms, raiders and wars pushed and pulled borders into more  stable configurations and money evolved into more universally accept  standards. More and more we saw precious metals like gold and silver  come to dominate what people saw as money. Then precious medals were  pressed into coins with fixed values. After coins, we started using little pieces of paper backed by stores of precious metals. Eventually we  cut the ties to precious metals all together, as countries went off the gold standard.
   Just as we saw chemical film evolve over a hundred years, getting more and more refined, only to get  suddenly and swiftly eclipsed by digital cameras, so too will paper cash suffer the same fate. It will get replaced by fluid, easily transferable digital money.
  The move to cashless societies started years ago. China is already a largely cashless society, a massive and swift change from only seven years ago when I visited in 2014 and cash was still king. Only seven years later,  86% of all people in China use mobile payments, skipping the ancient Visa and Mastercard PoS system in favor of ubiquitous QR codes.
 In late 2016, Prime Minister Narenda Modi  suddenly banned most of the country’s currency with the stroke of a pen. It was supposed to cut down on corruption and get people paying their taxes. They backtracked a year later but that’s only because they moved too fast. Australia tried  to ban using cash for purchases of 10K or more. The measure failed but these early failures don’t mean the war is won, just the battle.
 The ideas won’t go away and they’re a clear signal of how governments view cash:
   Because cash gives nations very little control. They can’t easily see where it goes and who has it and who spent it on what.
 Nation states have gotten very used to that control. Today nearly every person on Earth lives under the power of a nation state with a clear border, a coat of arms with special colors and symbols known as a flag, and a patriotic song. And as nation states became the ultimate power on the planet they seized control of the power to print money and eliminated  anyone else’s power to create it with laws and force.
 Initially, physical cash emblazed with the symbols of that nation state were the ultimate source of prestige. Having your own money meant you were a full fledged, independent power. Physical money was a source of pride for a nation.
  Above: Old Diner’s Club card — not related to me (I don’t think)
 But that started to change as money got more and more virtual. As it got  more virtual, it got more easily trackable. It lost its anonymity. As  credit cards rose to power in the 1950s and 1960s, money and where it was flowing became increasingly transparent, with physical ledgers held by private companies like Diner’s Club and Visa showing that movement in great detail. As computers and networks took the world by storm in the 1980s and 1990s, all those ledgers became digital and ever more transparent. Governments peered into those ledgers and controlling the movement of that money became even more powerful than printing the money itself.
 Now governments are used to seeing every transaction and where it goes and they don’t want to lose the power. Killing off cash would eliminate that anonymity forever. Nobody could so much as buy a lamp at a tag sale without them knowing about it.
 There’s just one problem. They can’t get rid of cash yet.
 As we saw in India, people revolted. They refused to turn the bills in and they used them anyway. After a year, India had to put the bills back  into circulation because their digital system wasn’t ready to handle the load. In Australia the pandemic crushed any delusion that Australia  already had a digital payment system strong enough to replace cash and  they knew small businesses would fight so they scraped the plan to stop  cash payments above 10k.
 Tofinally kill cash nation states need more than laws. People won’t accept the death of cash until they have a powerful alternative that has killer features they didn’t have with today’s money. A dominate technology doesn’t go away until a new one comes along with almost all the features of the old tech and a bunch of new ones nobody can live without.
       Most nation states were terrified of Bitcoin and the international, decentralized nature of it. Nation state leaders have enjoyed control of the money supply for so long that they can’t imagine an international currency like silk or salt anymore.
 Nation states reacted to cryptocurrency with fear and rage. When Facebook tried to launch  the Libra, congress and other world governments went after them. When  Telegram tried to build a fully scalable digital currency that could easily fly over encrypted messengers, the US government crushed them, despite the fact they raised all that money from accredited investors.
 But while the US and other governments were reacting in fear, China was watching blockchains closely and they understood their power before any other nation on Earth. While other superpowers were sleeping on blockchain, or laughing at Bitcoin, or  striking out at it in blind, animal fear, China got building.
 Of all the swirling variables that will kill off cash, it’s now China’s digital Yuan that will light the fuse that speeds us to the explosive and sudden death of cash worldwide, not laws or Bitcoin.
      They’re already  rolling out tests of the digital Yuan, doing airdrops and letting citizens buy things with free money. Based on videos and reviews I’ve seen, it’s a slick platform. It lets users exchange money whether they’re offline or not, just by touching phones. It syncs the transaction later.
 China was smart. Instead of seeing the wild price fluctuations of Bitcoin that had  idiotic western economists like Dr Doom railing against it, they saw a way to warp the initial libertarian design of Bitcoin to build a centrally controlled powerhouse of digital surveillance. They saw it as a way to build  a state sponsored system that can easily cross boarders, break through international sanctions with ease and eventually even smash the US dominated international banking system. They took the privacy preserving, decentralized foundations of blockchain and threw it out the window, replacing those idealistic traits with a tool of centralized control, a permission based, dictated “consensus” model that sets those original utopian ideals on fire.
 The Chinese also saw that a decentralized, borderless currency controlled by the hard to change rules of an algorithm meant governments could no longer devalue or inflate the money by removing some from circulation or printing more of it. They would lose the ability to impose currency controls or tell you how much you can take out at a time or how much you can send to someone else. Privacy focused cryptocurrencies were even worse, because it means everyone gets the same privacy, backed by ironclad cryptographic protocols that can’t be altered because investigators want to take a shortcut rather than do real detective work.
 China saw all that coming and wanted to get ahead of it before borderless money took over the world because by then it would be too late. So they moved fast. They banned Bitcoin exchanges in China and forced Bitcoin miners to register. Then they built a centrally controlled version of digital cash to swiftly crush decentralized money.
   They’ll seed the currency through their  belt and roads initiative, which has the Chinese investing heavily in developing economies, building roads, bridges and infrastructure or offering loans to do it. In no time, they’ll expect developing countries to pay back those loans using the digital Yuan. They might face some resistance at first, but eventually small countries that don’t have heavily built payment and banking infrastructure will see how simple and easy and painless it is and they’ll start to use it more and more often. They’ll also see the backwards, archaic and absurd system the US and Europe still uses for banking and skip it entirely.
 Want to send money through the western system? Write a note, sign it, scan it, and fax it to someone. Then maybe a day later someone gets to it. A few days later, your wire appears across the border, as long as the teller didn’t screw it up and the wire didn’t bounce back. Of course, that’s if someone in a third world country can even get a bank account in the first place, or if a small country business can even comply with all the  FACTA requirements if they want to do business with Americans.
 Soas they struggle with the archaic international banking system built with a thousand ill conceived and conflicting choke points, they’ll also fire up their digital Yuan wallet on their smart phone or use a beautiful digital dashboard on their desktop and they’ll zap money across the world in seconds using the Chinese system.
  While the US abandoned their leadership and investment in other countries, turning inward with a disgusting, selfish, absurd “ me first”  national populist view of the world, the Chinese were investing in the future. While Americans fight a pointless and destructive  culture war, the Chinese are out investing in 70+ different countries across the world, the ones with unstable governments and piss poor infrastructure. They’re buying the future with a few roads and bridges, while the US builds walls.
  Of course, the western world doesn’t want to see a Chinese nation currency come to dominate the emerging worlds of Africa and the far east. The other central banks of the world are racing to build their own digital currencies to counter the threat but they’re already incredibly far behind.  Italy wants to go totally cashless. So does Europe, which  intensified its work on a digital Euro. But western powers are up against a beta test of a live system in China, while they’re still drawing up plans on the white board.
 Western powers will eventually catch up though and soon we’ll have an all out arms race of nation state digital currencies, all vying for supremacy. Some of them will be hampered by horrible IT and programmers, some will  have terrible designs, some will screw up security and get hacked, but some of them will get it right and the nations that fail will simply adapt to copy the successes.
 And with those new central bank digital currencies (CBDC) countries have  full surveillance right in your pocket at all times at all times. Every transaction you ever make. Everywhere you ever go. All of it tied to your identity and history and geolocation data.
 A decade after that you won’t file your taxes, they’ll get yanked out automatically every time you buy a second hand toaster at a garage sale. If there’s a mistake, you’ll call to fight it or have an accountant try to get you a refund while the government takes a free loan from you for a year. The money won’t even pass from central banks to private banking institutions, it will go right into your pocket and those panopticoins will know everything you ever did from cradle to grave.
  And despite naive proclamations to the contrary in the crypto community, people will absolutely use those central bank currencies. Even more, they’ll be proud to do it.
  Of course, they’ll only be proud and happy when their government is in good working shape.
 When things are going right the abuses in the system won’t effect enough  people to rise above the public’s radar. Only the “bad guys” will pay the price, but probably not anyone that average citizens know personally, so the system will mostly work for most people. They won’t care about privacy because they’re just buying diapers for their kids and books on Amazon and coffee in the local shop.
 But when things go wrong in a government, like we’re seeing now in  the United States with the Capitol insurrection, their ideas will change fast. A nation state backed currency is only as good as the government that controls it. You have to trust it and in order to trust it you have to trust the people behind it.
  America is not a fixed thing. It’s a collective of people behind every  government agency that’s always in motion. IBM is a company but the company is always changing. That’s because the people behind it change. The Department of Justice isn’t the Department of Justice unless you ask which Department of Justice? What year? Who was running it? There is no Department of Justice, there is only the people running it at the time.
 Trust is based on who’s in power and what they do with that power.
  And trust is often broken. It only takes one breach of trust to shatter it  forever. A man might be loyal to his wife for ten years only to cheat on her and destroy all that trust in a single instant.
 “The root problem with conventional currency is all the trust that’s required to make it work. The central bank must be trusted not to debase the currency, but the history of fiat currencies is full of breaches of that trust,”  wrote Satoshi in 2009.
 When governments go crazy, they’ll now have the power to pass that crazy on to you. Want to take your hard earned money and move to another country with less crazy people in power? You can’t. Currency controls are now in place. Your money is frozen. Spend it in the country or nowhere else.
 When China eventually ties their  social credit score to their currency they’ll be able to stop someone from getting on a bus or buying anything but basic food because they expressed an opinion on WeChat that the Party didn’t like at all.
 Maybe you think all that’s a good thing? Shouldn’t a nation state hold the  power to control the flow of money in and out of the country? They’re just trying to protect their citizens and the economy from the devastating effects of a crisis.
 But you might feel differently if you lived in Iceland, which  adopted strict currency controls after the 2008 financial crisis and they didn’t lift them until 2017, a decade later. That meant that everyone living there was only under the illusion that they owned their money when they never really did.
 Want to buy a house overseas and move your family there with your hard  earned income because it holds the prospect of a better life? You can’t anymore. Want to buy shares in Tesla or another tech stock that’s going up 1000%? You can’t. You can only invest in local businesses that are suffering and making negligible or
......

（路透社）-据一名执法人员称，美国执法部门正在调查一名妇女是在1月6日围困国会大厦期间从美国众议院议长南希·佩洛西（Nancy Pelosi）的办公室拿了便携式计算机还是硬盘驱动器，并试图将设备出售给俄罗斯情报部门。法院备案。
  联邦调查局特工在周日晚间发布的宣誓书中披露了细节，该宣誓书概述了针对宾夕法尼亚州妇女莱利·六月·威廉姆斯（Riley June Williams）的刑事案件，该女子被指控非法违反国会大厦并将人们带往佩洛西的办公室。
 在美国总统唐纳德·特朗普的支持者围攻之后，国会办公室盗窃电子设备一直是一个令人担忧的问题。
 代理美国检察官迈克尔·舍温（Michael Sherwin）在袭击事件发生后说，一些盗窃案可能危害了他所说的“国家安全股”。
  根据向哥伦比亚特区美国地方法院提交的宣誓书，FBI收到了某人的提示，该人称他们曾是威廉姆斯的浪漫伴侣。
 宣誓书说，威廉姆斯说：“威廉姆斯打算将计算机设备发送给俄罗斯的一位朋友，后者然后打算将该设备出售给俄罗斯的外国情报服务SVR。”
 宣誓书说，据提示者说：“由于未知原因，计算机设备向俄罗斯的转让失败了，威廉姆斯仍然拥有或销毁了计算机设备。”调查仍在进行中。 
根据联邦调查局的说法，威廉姆斯似乎逃离了宾夕法尼亚州哈里斯堡附近的一个地址，她与母亲共享，取消了电话号码，并删除了社交媒体帐户。  国会大厦被围困两天后，汉米尔（Hammill）说，用于演讲的笔记本电脑从佩洛西（Pelosi）办公室的会议室被盗。 目前尚不清楚该装置是否是威廉姆斯被指控使用的那个装置。

(Reuters) - U.S. law enforcement is investigating whether a woman took a laptop computer or hard drive from U.S. House of Representatives Speaker Nancy Pelosi’s office during the Jan. 6 siege of the Capitol and tried to sell the device to Russian intelligence, according to a court filing.
  An FBI agent disclosed the detail in an affidavit released on Sunday night that outlined a criminal case against Riley June Williams, a Pennsylvania woman accused of unlawfully breaching the Capitol building and directing people to Pelosi’s office.
 The theft of electronic devices from congressional offices has been a persistent worry following the siege by supporters of U.S. President Donald Trump.
 Acting U.S. Attorney Michael Sherwin said after the attack that some of the thefts might have potentially jeopardized what he described as “national security equities.”
  According to the affidavit filed with the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia, the FBI received a tip from someone who stated they were a former romantic partner of Williams.
 The tipster said Williams “intended to send the computer device to a friend in Russia, who then planned to sell the device to SVR, Russia’s foreign intelligence service,” the affidavit stated.
 According to the tipster, “the transfer of the computer device to Russia fell through for unknown reasons and Williams still has the computer device or destroyed it,” the affidavit stated. The investigation remains open.
   According to the FBI, it appears Williams has fled an address near Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, that she shared with her mother, deactivated her phone number, and took down social media accounts.
  Two days after the Capitol siege, Hammill said a laptop used for presentations was stolen from a conference room in Pelosi’s office. It was unclear whether that device was the one Williams was accused of taking.
title: 联邦调查局说，女人可能试图向俄罗斯人出售佩洛西计算机设备
title_en: Woman may have tried to sell Pelosi computer device to Russians, FBI says

响应佛罗里达州发布的逮捕令，协助创建佛罗里达州COVID-19仪表板的数据科学家丽贝卡·琼斯（Rebekah Jones）已将自己告上了警察局。
  琼斯被控针对计算机，计算机系统，计算机网络和电子设备的用户的一项犯罪，＆＃34;佛罗里达州执法部门周一在一份声明中说。
  琼斯曾表示，在拒绝操纵数据表明佛罗里达州已准备好放宽冠状病毒限制之后，她失业了。琼斯说，她因继续发表关于州长罗恩·德桑蒂斯如何处理COVID-19的言论而受到惩罚，理由是她上个月被捕并对其房屋进行了突袭。
  FDLE在其声明中说，12月7日从搜查令中检索到的证据表明，琼斯非法访问了该系统，向大约1,750人发送了一条消息，并下载了机密FDOH数据并将其保存到她的设备中。
  据佛罗里达州调查员琼斯说＆＃34;找不到证据与官员说引发这次袭击的事件有关：在紧急警报平台上向规划小组发送的聊天消息，敦促人们公开谈论佛罗里达州的冠状病毒策略。她在周六在Twitter上写道，警察确实找到了我从该州或类似性质的来源收到/下载的文档。
    由NPR获得的逮捕令声称，琼斯于11月10日获得对州卫生部门软件的未经授权的访问权，并向阅读以下内容的员工发送了大量短信，现在是时候再说了17,000人死。您知道这是错误的。您不必参与其中。成为英雄。在为时已晚之前大声说出来。
  它说该消息引起了“怀疑和混乱”。共享该计划帐户的工作组中，并促使信息技术人员将注意力转移到解决可能的网络攻击上。逮捕令说，总的来说，为了解决这一事件，约有30名雇员不得不停止其日常工作。 
逮捕令还指称，琼斯下载并保存了卫生部门的数据，其中包含与佛罗里达州约19182人的联系信息。 —到她自己的设备，并尝试在11月12日再次访问该消息传递平台，但均未成功。
  琼斯新闻传出后佛罗里达州执法部门告诉NPR，在等待逮捕令出现之前，该机构正在与她的律师进行谈判，以安排将她拘留。
  琼斯周六说，她打算上交。后来，她被预订进入塔拉哈西的莱昂县拘留所。
    为了保护我的家人免受持续的警察暴力侵害，并表明我准备好与他们扔给我的东西作斗争，我周日晚上将自己变成了佛罗里达州的警察，＆＃34;她在推特上说。 ＆＃34;总督不会赢得他在科学和言论自由方面的战争。他不会沉默那些说话的人。＆＃34;
  琼斯（Jones）正面临三级重罪指控，他于周一早上在现场直播的听证会上以法官身份出庭。琼斯律师在听证会上说，她目前居住在华盛顿特区，开车两天才能上交。
  法官设定琼斯为保证金为2500美元，条件是她与据称已下载个人信息的任何人都没有联系，也没有通过佛罗里达州计算机系统的联系。他反对该州要求的GPS监视和禁止互联网访问的裁决。
  12月初，佛罗里达州执法人员没收了琼斯（Jones＆＃39;电脑，电话和其他设备在塔拉哈西的家中执行搜查令时。她后来提起诉讼，称突袭行动是特工对她的另一次报复，突袭行动是特工带着武器带入她的家。 
琼斯周六在Twitter上说，针对她的国家指控是在塔拉哈西法官告诉警方第二天后发布的，如果他们不调查犯罪，他们必须归还我的设备。 ＆＃34;  在琼斯被解雇后的几个月中，她创建了自己的仪表板来报告冠状病毒信息，称为佛罗里达COVID行动，提供有关测试选项的数据和信息。 当她启动该程序时，美国卫生部的一名发言人为该州的信息中心辩护，并建议琼斯（Jones）离开。 版本包含不可靠的数据。

Have you ever picked up something and wondered, 'what is that?' Taxonomists help answer that question by dutifully documenting phenotypic (trait) and genotypic (genetic) differences among living things that allow them to be quickly distinguished and identified. Placing organisms into categories is useful so that instead of describing a slew of characteristics, we can simply use broad categories as reference points to inform us not only about the nature of an individual, but also about its relationship to other similar organisms. A new organism classified as a vertebrate, for example, will be commonly understood to have a spine composed of vertebrae. For scientists, taxonomic groups are touchstones of understanding: a foundation upon which to build new knowledge. This metaphor communicates the fundamental importance of taxonomy, but it implies a stability that taxonomic classification lacks.      For much of scientific history, fungi have been a botanist’s domain. Until very recently  — reasonably within a human lifetime — fungi remained classified as plants as part of a centuries-old division that can be summed up by an axiom attributed to Carl Linnaeus: “Plants grow and live; Animals grow, live and feel.” This “father of modern taxonomy” (and  deviser of racist classifications of humans) classified living organisms into 2 categories: either animals or plants. This paradigm can be rephrased as animals and “not animals,” as the category “plants” long represented a ragtag group of unrelated organisms. Without the context of evolution, these classifications sought to place organisms by perceived, oberservable similarity, instead of “relatedness” in a modern, genetic sense.     Classifying fungi as plants has led to some curious events. The earliest description of fungi pathogenic to insects  (likely  Cordyceps militaris) by the French entomologist René Antoine Ferchault de Réaumur was as a plant root. The Mycological Society of America was established while fungi were still considered plants, and the society’s journal  Mycologia originated from the New York Botanical Garden. This garden continues to maintain one of the world’s largest collections of fungi in their herbarium. This pairing of fungi with plants is a present problem: misclassification matters because how we classify organisms affects how we understand, support (financially and culturally) and engage with them.   Today, we know that fungi are not plants, but the botanical history of fungi provides an interesting perspective on our scientific biases, on how we classify organisms and how these impact our collective knowledge.     Taxonomic classifications are in constant flux, as we refine our understanding of the incredible diversity that surrounds us. Even in the age of genomics, we have only just scratched the surface of this diversity. Because we don’t have a full picture of the diversity of life, our best laid classifications can be (and are) routinely shifted by a newcomer or fresh evidence. Today, we have the luxury of molecular tools for classification, but taxonomic classifications can be traced back before the discovery of DNA, the concept of evolution and the invention of the microscope. Early classifications were limited by the tools (and views) available to them.     We must keep this caveat in mind when examining some of the early attempts at classifying life. Mushrooms were the earliest representatives of fungi to be classified. Based on observations of mushrooms, early taxonomists determined that fungi are immobile (fungi are not immobile) and they have rigid cell walls that support them. These characteristics were sufficient for early scientists to determine that fungi are not animals and to lump them with plants.      We have arrived at our first reason fungi are not plants: fungi lack chloroplasts. This verdant, unifying feature of plants is readily observable to the eye, and these chlorophyll-containing plastids continue to be an important milestone for our modern understanding of plant evolution. Of course, there are plants that lack functional chloroplasts, such as  ghost pipes (Monotropa), but we know these flowering plants (“higher plants,” once upon a time) lost chlorophyll during their evolutionary history. This evolutionary context was lacking until Darwin came along, but demonstrates how callously uncooperative biology is with our artificial delineations. Broad outlines for our categories for living things were based on what we could see, and microbes, including fungi without a fruiting body to observe, were an afterthought.   Old paradigms for classifying life were so ingrained that challenging them was a difficult task. Still, the various groups of fungi provided scientists with a nice tool for the task. In 1955, George Willard Martin challenged the notion that fungi should be classified as plants with  an article entitled “Are fungi plants?”. In the introduction, he hazarded a guess that most mycologists at the time would answer ‘yes.’ Still, his thorough examination of the topic influenced Robert Harding Whittaker in his pursuit to revolutionize taxonomy.      Whittaker published several articles proposing more kingdoms of life. He eventually settled on 5 kingdoms, but he was engaged in a philosophical, decades-long debate on the appropriate way to catalogue life. While a contemporary taxonomist Herbert Copeland argued for detailed description of features for classification informed by historical understanding,  Whittaker advanced his theory based on ecology. Whittaker’s theory was  based on 3 types of ecological roles organisms can play: producers (photosynthesizers), consumers (eaters) and reducers (decomposers).       Arguably, Whittaker’s reasoning finally extricated fungi from the kingdom of plants, and so it is our next reason fungi are not plants: fungi have a unique mode of acquiring nutrients. Fungi secrete digestive enzymes, then absorb nutrients from their surroundings. This is in sharp contrast to plants, which make their own food (thanks to their chloroplasts). It was clear to Whittaker that this difference distinguished fungi from plants ecologically, but he was also grappling with a more basic question: why are we classifying organisms?  Is it better to try to unify organisms by evolutionary history than to divide them?     When the classification of living organisms was first undertaken, we believed the catalog could one day be complete. Whittaker knew that new editions of this catalog were produced each day, so instead of basing taxonomy on features alone, he argued for kingdoms that represented major evolutionary trajectories. These categories would be more useful for evolutionary and ecological questions. He published his  textbook-ready 5 kingdom classification in 1969, which included separate fungal and plant kingdoms. Reason 3: Molecular Evidence Demonstrates Fungi Are More Closely Related to Animals Than to Plants
  The proposed separation of fungi and plants is indisputably supported by molecular evidence.  Computational phylogenetics comparing eukaryotes revealed that fungi are more closely related to us than to plants. Fungi and animals form a clade called opisthokonta, which is named after a single, posterior flagellum present in their last common ancestor. Today, this posterior flagellum propels primitive fungal spores and animal sperm alike.      This is our final reason fungi are not plants: the best available  molecular evidence demonstrates fungi are more closely related to animals than plants. These computational and molecular approaches are convincing because they provide robust evolutionary histories that indicate organismal relationships and estimate when they diverged from common ancestors. A molecular understanding of life has uncovered 3 possible major domains of life: Bacteria, Archaea and Eukarya ( nested within Archaea). These are distinguished by cellular components (e.g., membrane-bound organelles) and the composition of the cell membrane.     Although they’ve been granted their own kingdom, fungi continue to demand taxonomic attention. Molecular approaches reveal that mycologists have described some fungi more than once. Various names for sexual (i.e., producing mushrooms) and asexual forms of the same fungus have inspired an effort to revise fungal taxa, humbly called  “One Name = One Fungus.” This initiative continues today, but the challenge is immense, with databases like  Index Fungorum listing synonyms and citations with descriptions of fungi.     What has the (incorrect) classification of mycology as a botanical pursuit done to the advancement of the field? The more we know about fungi, the better prepared we are to protect ourselves (and other organisms) from fungal infections. Fungi have so much to uniquely teach us about (to name just 3 examples) evolution, ecology and cellular biology. Plant science departments continue to train many mycologists across the country, but where would mycology be if this discipline were supported with a similar number of departments? Would more microbiome studies explicitly include the mycobiome? Would we be better prepared for  fungal threats to food security if the U.S. Department of Agriculture instead had a Animal, Plant *and Fungi* Health Protection Service? We have much to learn about fungi, but one thing is for certain: fungi are not plants.
title: 真菌不是植物的三个原因
title_en: Three reasons fungi are not plants

note:
比尔·马赫（Bill Maher）的智慧之言，他当然不对唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）表示同情。它已经两天大了，但是他周五的实时片段的总结解决了一些重要问题，特别是在不久的将来。 8100万拜登选民与7400万特朗普选民如何相处，反之亦然？马赫认为，试图将所有特朗普选民描绘成叛乱分子，是媒体和政治上最糟糕的策略，他是正确的。
 Maher为7400万的辩护补充道：“您可以像一个笨拙的人一样喜欢某个东西，而不必一个人。”通过RealClearPolitics的朋友Ian Schwartz，使用温和的NSFW语言：
   比尔·马哈尔（Bill Maher）呼吁民主党人不要将所有特朗普选民视为可悲的，并要求共和党人在周五HBO节目“实时”中发表关于特朗普选民“悲剧”的独白中，考虑他们的某些愤怒是否被误导了。
 “不要让5,000人与7400万人混淆，”马赫对观众说。 “是的，即使在精神上支持起义也是令人遗憾的。但是，坚持自由信仰与对他们采取暴力行动是有区别的。至少他们总是告诉我有关伊斯兰恐怖主义的事情。”
 主持人说：“我一直在努力思考这个节目，这是一个棘手的问题，即我们所有人美国人如何学会与一个无法忍受的混蛋分享一个国家，” “我鼓吹，而且仍然这样做，你可以讨厌特朗普，但不是所有喜欢他的人。而且，尽管看起来似乎违反直觉，但您可能喜欢混蛋而不是自己一个人。”
 马赫对在国会大厦被解雇时被枪杀的一名暴徒确实表示了令人惊讶的同情，但并非基于任何潜在的警察渎职行为。马赫建议，阿什利·巴比特（Ashli​​ Babbitt）不应一开始就被人误认为会参与其中，但随后指出，她在深蓝色状态下的生活也不尽如人意。
 “阿什利·巴比特（Ashli​​ Babbitt）是否应该采用更好的逻辑，以确保生活中真正的问题不是恋童癖的民主党人或安提法，而伊丽莎白·沃伦（Elizabeth Warren）这样的人实际上至少是在为掠夺性贷款和经济剥削做些什么？是。但是，并非所有人都能整天观看MSNBC。她还有其他优先事项，例如在海外作战。”他说。 
“也许，由于所有政治都是地方性的，她只知道自己生活在一个州，她更在乎她的有毒的白皙而不是有毒的破碎，”马赫补充说。 “而且这个国家完全由民主党人掌管。是的他们也没有阻止任何人收取她169％的贷款利息。”
 马赫继续说：“美国到处都是厌烦的不高兴的人，他们只是想打破狗屎，这不足为奇。” “特朗普肯定不会流失任何沼泽，但是当涉及贪污腐败和每个人都弄湿他的喙时，加利福尼亚，是的，这也是沼泽。我们不能以低于50万美元的价格建造无家可归者的住房，也不能以每英里2亿美元的价格修建连接国家的铁路。”
 Maher在加利福尼亚沼泽方面有一些个人经验，他将在结尾进行介绍。也许这就是为什么马希尔对巴比特有更多的同情心，并在骚动中扩大了其他一些人，同时仍然谴责他们的参与。 Maher对因各种不同原因投票支持特朗普，根本不希望起义的7400万人感到同情的原因之一。他显然不同意这种选择，但很好地理解了过去，并阐明了为什么其他所有人也应该这样做。
 要抓住这一点，人们将必须停止使政治成为人类的必要条件，这一点对所有方面都是正确的。人们不得不怀疑，马赫自己的听众中有多少人愿意这样做，但是对马赫挑战他们和其他所有人感到敬佩。
note_en:
Words of wisdom from Bill Maher, who certainly doesn’t express any sympathy for or with Donald Trump within them. It’s a couple of days old, but the wrap-up of his  Real Time episode on Friday addresses some important points, especially in the near future. How do 81 million Biden voters get along with 74 million Trump voters, and vice versa? Maher argues that attempting to paint all Trump voters as insurrectionists is about the worst possible strategy for the media and in politics, and he’s correct.
 And Maher adds in defense of the 74 million: “You can like something run by a****les without being one yourself.” Via our friend Ian Schwartz at  RealClearPolitics, with mildly NSFW language:
   Bill Maher called on Democrats not to write off all Trump voters as deplorable and asks Republicans to consider whether some of their anger is misdirected in a monologue delivered about the “tragedy” of Trump voters Friday on his HBO show “Real Time.”
 “Let’s not confuse 5,000 people with 74 million,” Maher said to his audience. “Yes, even supporting the insurrection in spirit is, well, deplorable. But there’s a difference between holding illiberal beliefs and acting violently on them. At least that’s what they always told me about Islamic terrorism.”
 “I keep wrestling on this show with the hard question of how do Americans, all of us, learn to share a country with assholes you can’t stand,” the host said. “I preach, and still do, you can hate Trump, but not all the people who like him. And as counterintuitive as it may seem, you can like something run by assholes without being one yourself.”
 Maher did have some surprising sympathy for the one rioter shot and killed in the sacking of the Capitol, but not on the basis of any potential police malfeasance. Ashli Babbitt shouldn’t have been there in the first place and had been horribly misled into participation, Maher suggests, but then points out that her life in a deep-blue state hadn’t exactly been going well either.
 “Should Ashli Babbitt have applied better logic in seeing that her real problem in life wasn’t pedophile Democrats or Antifa and that people like Elizabeth Warren were actually the ones who were at least trying to do something about predatory lending and economic exploitation? Yes. But not everyone can watch MSNBC all day. She had other priorities, like fighting overseas,” he said.
 “Maybe, since all politics is local, all she knew was she lives in a state that cares more about her toxic whiteness than her toxic brokeness,” Maher added. “And that the state that is run entirely by Democrats. Yeah. They didn’t stop anyone from charging her 169% interest on a loan either.”
 “It shouldn’t be that surprising that America is full of fed-up unhappy people who just want to break shit,” Maher continued. “Trump sure didn’t drain any swamps but when it comes to graft and corruption and everybody wetting his beak, California, yeah, that’s a swamp too. We can’t put up a housing unit for the homeless for less than $500,000 or build a rail line connecting the state for less than $200 million a mile.”
 Maher has some personal experience with the California swamp, which he covers toward the end. Perhaps that’s why Maher finds more empathy with Babbitt and by extension some of the others at the riot while still condemning their participation. It’s certainly one reason why Maher feels empathy toward 74 million people who voted for Trump for all sorts of different reasons and had no desire for insurrection at all. He clearly disagrees with that choice, but sees past that very well and articulates why everyone else should, too.
 To grasp this, people will have to stop making politics the  sine qua non of humanity, a point that is true for all sides. One has to wonder how many in Maher’s own audience is willing to do that, but kudos to Maher for challenging them and everyone else.
title: 不要将5K骚乱者与7400万选民混淆

note:
Deliveroo又从包括亚马逊在内的股东那里获得了1.8亿美元（1.32亿英镑）的资金，此举使这家外卖公司的市值超过了70亿美元，因为它正准备在股票交易所上市。
 Deliveroo的创始人兼首席执行官Will Shu表示，这家总部位于伦敦的公司将利用现有投资者的新现金来改善“消费者，骑士和餐馆”的业务，这是他首次证实该公司正在制定计划。进行首次公开​​募股（IPO）。
  Deliveroo是受益于大流行相关的消费者支出变化的互联网公司之一，该公司表示，新一轮的投资者融资使该公司的估值“超过70亿美元”。
 成功的上市将使公司从大流行引起的最初冲击中恢复过来。
 去年四月，竞争与市场管理局（CMA）临时批准了亚马逊收购Deliveroo 16％的股份，此前该外卖送货服务警告称，由于首次锁定期间餐厅关闭导致“收入大幅下降”，亚马逊有可能倒闭。
 前投资银行家Shu表示，这笔现金被用于“新技术工具，以支持餐厅，为骑手提供更多工作并为客户提供更多选择”，他于2013年在伦敦的公寓里开始经营这项业务。股东看到了我们面前的机遇和增长潜力。”
 该公司计划扩展其仅配送厨房站点网络（称为“深色厨房”），杂货店交付和订阅服务，并允许更多餐厅通过自己的网站接单。 
城市投资者渴望将钱投入互联网公司，例如9月上市的The Hut Group，他们从在线支出的大幅增长中受益。这种胃口促使其他在线公司，包括贺卡和礼品零售商Moonpig，宣布了出售股票的计划。
 上周，Deliveroo的竞争对手Just Eat表示，由于冠状病毒的限制使人们无法在家，英国的订单在2020年的最后三个月增长了400％。
 Shu在12月发表讲话时说，Covid-19已将消费者对送餐服务的采用速度提高了大约两到三年，目前英国和爱尔兰的订单量是2019年的两倍。他说：“我们已经证明了我们的模式是有利可图的。”他补充说，以前这家亏损的公司“已经在经营水平上盈利了六个月以上”。
 Shu补充说，该公司已经看到“加入该平台的新客户数量有了惊人的增长”。 “我们还看到我们现有的客户希望订购更多，也为家庭订购更多，我们看到平均购物篮尺寸增加，订购的产品也更多。”
 CMA于2019年开始对亚马逊对该公司的投资进行深入调查，它担心这会使顾客，餐馆和杂货店面临更高的价格和更低质量的服务。 Deliveroo没有说明每位投资者在最新一轮融资中的出资额，但因此亚马逊的股份并未增加。 CMA在其调查决定不会导致竞争大幅减少之后，于夏季正式批准了该交易。
 •本文于2021年1月18日进行了修改，以阐明CMA并未像最初所说的那样，在4月份通过亚马逊购买Deliveroo的16％股权而动摇。它已于4月临时清除，并于夏季进行了正式清除。
note_en:
Deliveroo has secured another $180m (£132m) of funding from shareholders including Amazon, in a move that values the takeaway delivery company at more than $7bn as it gears up for a blockbuster stock exchange listing.
 Will Shu, Deliveroo’s founder and chief executive, said the London-based company would use the new cash from existing investors to improve the business for “consumers, riders and restaurants”, as he confirmed for the first time that the company was working on plans for an initial public offering (IPO).
  Deliveroo is among the internet firms to benefit from big pandemic-related shifts in consumer spending, and the company said the new round of investor funding had valued the company at “over $7bn”.
 A successful stock market flotation would complete the company’s recovery from the initial shock caused by the pandemic.
 Last April the  Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) provisionally cleared Amazon’s purchase of a  16% stake in Deliveroo after the takeaway delivery service warned it could collapse after restaurant closures during the first lockdown resulted in a “significant decline in revenues”.
 The cash is being spent on “new tech tools to support restaurants, provide riders with more work and extend choice for customers”, said Shu, a former investment banker who launched the business from his London flat in 2013. “We are really pleased our shareholders see the opportunity and growth potential ahead of us.”
 The company plans to expand its network of delivery-only kitchen sites – called  “dark kitchens” – grocery delivery and subscription services, as well as allowing more restaurants to take orders via their own websites.
 City investors are eager to put their money into internet companies – such as the  The Hut Group, which floated in September – who are benefiting from big increases in online spending. That appetite has encouraged other online companies, including the greetings card and gift retailer  Moonpig, to announce plans to sell shares.
 Last week Deliveroo  rival Just Eat said UK orders had jumped 400% in the final three months of 2020 as coronavirus restrictions kept people at home.
 Speaking in December, Shu said Covid-19 has accelerated consumer adoption of food delivery services by about two to three years, with order volumes in the UK and Ireland currently running at double 2019 levels. “We’ve demonstrated our model is profitable,” he said, adding that the previously loss-making company had been “profitable at the operating level for over six months now”.
 Shu added that the company had seen an “incredible increase in new customers joining the platform”. “We also saw our existing customers looking to order more often, also ordering for the family more frequently, we saw average basket sizes increase, and also ordering a wider range of products.”
 In 2019 the CMA had embarked on an in-depth investigation into Amazon’s  investment in the company which it feared could leave customers, restaurants and grocers facing higher prices and lower quality services. Deliveroo did not state how much each investor had contributed in the latest funding round but Amazon’s stake has not increased as a result. The deal was formally cleared by the CMA in the summer after its probe decided it would not result in a substantial lessening of competition.
 • This article was amended on 18 January 2021 to clarify that the CMA did not wave through Amazon’s purchase of a 16% stake in Deliveroo in April as originally stated. It was provisionally cleared in April and given formal clearance in the summer.

三星的领导人李在y（又名周杰伦）再次入狱。 Lee因其在＆＃34;中的角色而卷入一场法律诉讼。选择，＆＃34; 2016年的一次重大韩国政治丑闻导致韩国总统朴槿惠被弹each并免职。丑闻以总统的最高援助而命名，崔顺锡（Choi Soon-sil）是萨满教派的成员，被发现通过对总统的影响来策划韩国政府的政策。这是一个很长的故事，但崔因涉嫌兜售他人而被判处20年徒刑，朴智星被判25年徒刑。
 三星在这场混乱中的一部分涉及指控李某贿赂崔某，因为与两家三星子公司合并有关的有利裁决。李在2017年被判贿赂，挪用公款，资本外逃和伪证罪后被判处五年徒刑。判刑六个月后，上诉法院将李的判决减半，并暂停了贿赂和挪用公款的指控。李被释放出狱，而上诉程序仍在继续。此案一直持续到韩国最高法院，该法院于2019年下令重审。
 今天的判决判处李某有期徒刑30个月，在已经服刑一年之后，三星的领导人应该在下一年的监狱里度过。
     Lee最初的定罪和不断减少的处罚对于被指控在韩国犯罪的三星高管来说是同等重要的。三星是如此之大，以至于每年占韩国GDP的10％至20％，因此担心该公司会对韩国经济造成何种损害。李刚去世的父亲，三星集团董事长李坤熙于1996年因贿赂罪名成立，2009年因逃税被定罪，但从未被捕或入狱。后来，总统赦免消除了他的犯罪记录。正如一位国会议员在李的刑罚于2018年减刑时所说的那样，我们再次确认三星凌驾于法律和法院之上。”
 彭博（Bloomberg）关于量刑的报告使我们对“亲三星”的看法有所了解。韩国各派对法院的裁决感到满意。该报告援引了淑明女子大学经济学名誉教授申世东（Shin Se-don）的话：“李光耀也许可以从监狱里管理这家公司，但会遇到一些挫折。李的入狱将使人们产生情感上的震惊。三星是我们经济的骨干力量，人们对结果感到不满。”
 李的父亲三星董事长李坤熙（Lee Kun-hee）于10月去世后，三星集团目前正在进行重大转型。 Lee Jae-yong多年来一直是三星的事实上的领导者，此前在心脏病发作后于2014年Lee Kun-hee住院治疗之后。现在，李在容将不得不适当地从＆＃34;副主席＆＃34;上升。向三星官方董事长致辞，并应对向三星帝国支付韩国50％的遗产税的财务挑战。彭博社（Bloomberg）报告说过渡很可能是推迟到李出狱。
note_en:
It's  back to jail for Samsung's leader, Lee Jae-yong (aka Jay Y. Lee). Lee has been embroiled in a legal battle for his role in " Choigate," a major 2016 South Korean political scandal that led to South Korean President Park Geun-hye being impeached and removed from office. The scandal is named for the president's top aid, Choi Soon-sil, a member of a shamanistic cult that was found to be masterminding South Korean government policy via her influence over the president. It's a long story, but Choi was sentenced to 20 years in prison for influence peddling, and Park was sentenced to 25 years.
 Samsung's part in this mess involves accusations that Lee bribed Choi for favorable rulings related to the merger of two Samsung affiliates. In 2017, Lee  was sentenced to five years in jail after being found guilty of bribery, embezzlement, capital flight, and perjury charges. Six months after his sentencing, an appeals court cut Lee's sentence in half and suspended the charges for bribery and embezzlement. Lee was  released from prison while the appeals process continued. The case wound its way all the way up to the South Korean Supreme Court, which, in 2019, ordered a retrial.
 Today's ruling sentences Lee to 30 months in prison, and after having already served one year, Samsung's leader should be spending the next year and a half behind bars.
     Lee's initial conviction and the constant whittling down of punishment has been par for the course for Samsung executives accused of crimes in South Korea. Samsung is so large that it makes up anywhere from 10 to 20 percent of South Korea's GDP, depending on the year, so there is a fear of what damaging the company would do to South Korea's economy. Lee's recently deceased father and the chairman of Samsung Group, Lee Kun-hee, was convicted of bribery in 1996 and of tax evasion in 2009 but was never arrested nor served jail time. Later, presidential pardons wiped out his criminal record. As one National Assembly member put it when Lee's sentence was reduced in 2018, "We confirmed once again that Samsung is above the law and the court.”
 Bloomberg's report on the sentencing gives us an idea of how the "Pro-Samsung" faction of South Korea feels about the court ruling. The report  quotes Shin Se-don, an emeritus professor of economics at Sookmyung Women’s University: “Lee might be able to manage the company from the jail, but there will be some setback. The jailing of Lee will give an emotional shock to the people. Samsung is a backbone of our economy and people will be upset about the result.”
 Samsung Group is currently undergoing a major transition after the death of Lee's father, Samsung Chairman Lee Kun-hee, in October. Lee Jae-yong has been the de facto leader of Samsung for years now, following Lee Kun-hee's hospitalization in 2014 after a heart attack. Now Lee Jae-yong will have to properly ascend from "vice-chairman" to official Samsung chairman and deal with the financial challenge of paying South Korea's 50 percent estate tax on the Samsung empire. Bloomberg reports that the transition is "likely" to be delayed until Lee gets out of prison.
title: 三星高管因贿赂被判入狱30个月
title_en: Samsung’s top executive gets 30 months in prison for bribery

9to5Mac报道称，苹果已经填补了一个漏洞，允许用户将iOS和iPad应用程序侧面加载到从未打算在台式机上运行的M1 Mac上。服务器端的更改可确保仅运行应用程序开发人员标记为针对Mac优化的应用程序。
 去年下半年，苹果推出了首批运行在其自己的基于ARM的定制CPU（称为M1）上的Mac，而不是已经在Mac中使用了几年的英特尔芯片。这些新机器包括入门级13英寸MacBook Pro，MacBook Air和低端Mac mini。
  由于这些机器现在与iPhone和iPad共享一种体系结构，而iPhone和iPad也具有密切相关的基于ARM的芯片，因此可以在装有M1芯片的Mac上本地运行iOS和iPadOS应用程序。苹果公司通过列出在Mac App Store上通过了自动测试的iPhone和iPad应用程序来支持这一点，只要开发人员没有选择退出该应用程序。但是，许多开发人员选择退出的原因有很多：因为他们认为该应用程序无法在笔记本电脑或台式机上提供良好的用户体验;因为它们提供了访问Mac上的服务或内容的首选替代方法；因为他们没有时间支持其他平台；或任何其他原因。
    在这种情况下，这些应用程序不会出现在Mac App Store中。但是几个月前，Reddit用户共享了一种在M1 Mac上侧加载这些应用的方法，方法是使用Mac的iMazing等第三方软件从连接的iOS或iPadOS设备中获取应用的IPA文件。
 不过，据9to5Mac称，Apple现在已经甩掉了必要的服务器端交换机。阻止此方法。该更改已经影响到运行macOS Big Sur 11.1的Mac，它也适用于运行11.2 beta的Mac。实际上，它甚至在后者上提供了一条错误消息：＆＃34;无法安装此应用程序，因为开发人员不希望它在此平台上运行。
 苹果可能会引入此更改有多种原因。例如，上述IPA文件方法的替代版本也可以用于加载盗版应用程序，而不是合法或合法安装在iOS或iPadOS设备上的文件中的文件。此外，Apple和开发人员可能会觉得这些应用程序在macOS上提供了较差的用户体验，可能会给支持或安全带来麻烦。
note_en:
Apple has plugged a hole that allowed users to sideload iOS and iPad applications to M1 Macs that were never intended to run on desktop, 9to5Mac  reports. The server-side change ensures that only applications that app developers have flagged as optimized for Mac will run.
 Late last year, Apple launched its first Macs running on its own ARM-based custom CPU called the M1, as opposed to the Intel chips that have been used in Macs for several years. These new machines included the entry-level 13-inch MacBook Pro, the MacBook Air, and the low-end Mac mini.
  Since those machines now share an architecture with iPhones and iPads, which also have closely related ARM-based chips, it became possible to  run iOS and iPadOS apps natively on Macs that were equipped with the M1 chip. Apple supported this by listing iPhone and iPad apps that passed an automated test on the Mac App Store, provided developers did not opt out of having the app listed. However, many developers  did opt out for any number of reasons: because they did not feel the app provided a good user experience on laptops or desktops; because they offer preferred alternative ways to access services or content on Macs; because they don't have the time to support an additional platform; or any number of other reasons.
    In those cases, the apps did not appear on the Mac App Store. But a couple of months ago, a  Reddit user shared a way of sideloading those apps on M1 Macs by fetching the app's IPA file from a connected iOS or iPadOS device using third-party software, like iMazing, for Macs.
 According to 9to5Mac, though, Apple has now "flipped the necessary server-side switch" to block this method. The change already affects Macs running macOS Big Sur 11.1, and it also applies to Macs running the 11.2 beta. In fact, it even offers an error message on the latter: "This application cannot be installed because the developer did not intend for it to run on this platform."
 There are a number of reasons Apple may have introduced this change. For one, an alternate version of the IPA file method described above could also be used to sideload pirated versions of apps rather than files from versions legitimately and legally installed on iOS or iPadOS devices. Further, Apple and developers may feel that these applications provide a poor user experience on macOS, and they could be a support or security headache.
title: Apple取消了用户找到的方法的插件，以在Mac上横向加载iOS应用
title_en: Apple pulls the plug on user-found method to sideload iOS apps on Mac

周日下午，Virgin Orbit加入了这家稀有的公司俱乐部，这些公司私人开发了火箭，并成功地将其发射入了轨道。此外，由于其LauncherOne火箭从747飞机上掉落，这家总部位于加利福尼亚州的公司已成为首家使用空中发射的液体燃料火箭进入轨道的公司。
  ＆＃34;宏伟的飞行是多年努力工作的结晶，也将在通往轨道的道路上释放出新一代的创新者，公司创始人理查德·布兰森爵士说。 ＆＃34;处女轨道取得了许多人认为不可能实现的目标。
 周日的飞行包括多次发射LauncherOne的上层发动机，并成功为NASA部署了几颗小卫星，这限制了一项历时约八年的开发计划，并带来了众多技术挑战。
 与从地面发射的传统助推器相比，空中发射的火箭具有一些优势，最显着的是可以灵活地到达不同的轨道，并且能够在相当恶劣的天气下起飞。但是，要获得这些好处，维珍轨道公司必须设计一种液体燃料火箭，该火箭可以从飞机上水平降下，点燃发动机，并迅速将自己定向到更垂直的轨道上。 （尽管轨道科学公司在1980年代后期开发了飞马火箭从运载飞机上降下，但它是使用固体推进剂的更直接的设计。）
  从飞机上掉下的火箭由于飞机和飞行员的接近而无法立即点燃发动机。对于LauncherOne，火箭的NewtonThree发动机在掉落后3.25秒被点燃。主机启动时间为5.2秒。在这段时间里，火箭在下降，并失去了从飞​​机在约30,000英尺处获得的速度。
  由于这种阻力，负加速作用在助推器上，从而对火箭的结构及其推进系统造成各种问题。一个问题是，这开始迫使液态氧和煤油推进剂推向燃料箱的顶部，而残量气体（随着推进剂的消耗而充满燃料箱）则流向发动机进气口。
 点火过程本身也是空气中的挑战。在地面上，火箭通常会点燃发动机，并且在火箭被释放之前，机载计算机会执行最终的快速检查，以确保一切正常。这就是为什么升空通常在点火后几秒钟。一号发射器没有错误的余地，因为如果不点火，火箭只会掉入海中。 
该公司及其工程师通过火箭的设计能够克服所有这些问题，甚至更多。 但是这花了时间和很多钱。 布兰森承认，他和其他投资者已向维珍轨道公司投入了约10亿美元，这笔钱可投资于小型卫星发射器，无论它有多创新。 去年，Ars探索了Virgin Orbit实现盈利的途径，这条路并不容易。  但这是另一天的讨论。 周日，维珍轨道号（Virgin Orbit）进行了第二次飞行，似乎是一个完美无瑕的任务。 很少有公司使用私人开发的飞行器来做到这一点，实际上，除了轨道科学，SpaceX和Rocket Lab以外，很少有公司这样做。 这是一个美好的一天。
note_en:
On Sunday afternoon, Virgin Orbit joined the rare club of companies that have privately developed a rocket and successfully launched it into orbit. Moreover, with its LauncherOne rocket dropped from a 747 aircraft, the California-based company has become the first to reach orbit with an air-launched, liquid-fueled rocket.
  "This magnificent flight is the culmination of many years of hard work and will also unleash a whole new generation of innovators on the path to orbit,"  said Sir Richard Branson, the founder of the company. "Virgin Orbit has achieved something many thought impossible."
 Sunday's flight, which included multiple firings of LauncherOne's upper stage engine and successful deployment of several small satellites for NASA, caps a development program that has spanned about eight years and myriad technical challenges.
 An air-launched rocket has some advantages over traditional boosters launched from the ground, most notably flexibility in reaching different orbits and the ability to take off in fairly inclement weather. However, to obtain these benefits, Virgin Orbit had to design a liquid-fueled rocket that could be dropped horizontally from an aircraft, ignite its engines, and rapidly orient itself into a more vertical trajectory. (Although Orbital Sciences developed the Pegasus rocket to drop from a carrier aircraft in the late 1980s, it was a more straightforward design using solid propellant.)
  A rocket dropped from an aircraft cannot ignite its engines immediately due to the proximity of the plane and its pilots. In the case of LauncherOne, the rocket's NewtonThree engine is ignited 3.25 seconds after being dropped. Main engine start comes at 5.2 seconds. During this time, the rocket is falling and losing the velocity it gained from the aircraft at about 30,000 feet.
  Due to this drag, a negative acceleration acts on the booster, causing all sorts of problems for both the rocket's structure and its propulsion system. One problem is that this begins to force the liquid oxygen and kerosene propellants to the top of the tanks and the ullage gas—which fills the tanks as propellant is expended—toward the engine inlet.
 The ignition process itself is also a challenge in the air. On the ground, a rocket typically ignites its engines, and the onboard computer performs a final, quick check to make sure everything is healthy, before the rocket is released. This is why liftoff typically follows ignition by a few seconds. There is no margin for error with Launcher One, because if ignition does not happen, the rocket simply falls into the ocean.
  The company and its engineers were able to overcome all of these issues and more with the design of their rocket. But it took time and a lot of money. Branson has acknowledged that he and other investors have put about $1 billion into Virgin Orbit, which is a lot of money to invest in a small satellite launcher, however innovative it could be.  Ars explored Virgin Orbit's pathways toward profitability last year, and the road will not be easy.
 But those are discussions for another day. On Sunday, Virgin Orbit reached orbit on just its second flight, with what appeared to be a pretty much flawless mission. Few companies have done this with privately developed vehicles—very few indeed beyond Orbital Sciences, SpaceX, and Rocket Lab. It was a good day.
title: Virgin Orbit刚刚赢得了其名称的轨道部分
title_en: Virgin Orbit just earned the orbit part of its name

嗨，朋友们和新读者们，欢迎回到The Station，这是一份新闻通讯，专门介绍人们和包裹从A点到B点的所有当前和未来方式。
 在发布本周新闻之前，让我们先做一些整理工作。首先，您可能已经注意到，The Station在星期日而不是星期六登陆您的电子邮件收件箱。

 您何时想看车站？您对新闻通讯有何喜好？
 最后一项：我现在是TechCrunch的交通编辑。标题更改带来更多的责任和使命。我将邀请更多的自由职业者来扩大我们的“交通运输的未来”范围。调查记者马克·哈里斯（Mark Harris）已经为我们提供了许多精彩的文章，他将在这里更加固定。哈里斯（Harris）善于铲除隐藏在法律文件和文件中的新闻，例如他在2019年发布的特斯拉关税条款，以及对埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）的拉斯维加斯环路项目的乘客容量的见解。
 我希望在接下来的几周和几个月内为运输局增加更多面孔。
 给我发电子邮件kirsten.korosec@techcrunch.com，以分享想法，批评，提出意见或提示。您也可以在Twitter上直接给我发送邮件-@kirstenkorosec。 
也许这是一种虚拟格式，但自动驾驶技术在今年的CES上却没有像过去那样扮演主角。
 取而代之的是，其他几个主题出现在CES上，主要围绕信息娱乐和高级驾驶员辅助系统。并延续2020年的趋势，出现了几款巨大的屏幕，包括上图所示的梅赛德斯超级屏幕。
 先锋，哈曼和松下都透露了未来的产品，旨在将更多的音频和视频技术引入车辆。例如，哈曼（Harman）推出了三个新的“体验概念”，它们可以将车辆中的信息娱乐系统变成音乐厅，录音室或游戏中心。
 松下还宣布与英国初创公司Envisics建立合作关系，共同开发和商业化用于汽车，卡车和SUV的新一代平视显示器。平视显示器或HUD似乎无处不在。该技术不是新技术，但最近的进步推动了这些系统的功能，这些系统已集成在车辆仪表板中，并将图像投影到挡风玻璃上，以帮助驾驶员导航并提供其他警报。
  通用汽车可能在2021年虚拟现实CES上占有最大的份额，至少在运输领域是这样。这家汽车制造商选择在技术贸易展览会上宣布一个名为BrightDrop的新业务部门，该部门将专注于商用车以及面向商业市场的其他产品和服务。但这还不是全部。
 通用汽车借此机会挑逗了即将上市的雪佛兰Bolt EUV，该车将采用通用汽车的免提高速公路驾驶辅助技术，称为Super Cruise，以及凯迪拉克Celestiq仪表板甚至一个新徽标。这一系列公告的目的很明确：通用汽车希望世界以及股东们知道它对电气化和互联汽车技术的重视。
 通用汽车的众多公告不容错过-甚至还有一个eVTOL。相反，Mobileye的公告在雷达之下飞了一些，但可以说是值得注意的。 
Mobileye概述了将自动驾驶汽车测试扩展到更多城市的计划，这是预期的，并且与该公司先前宣布的计划一致。
 对我而言，最突出的是Mobileye总裁兼首席执行官Amnon Shashua进行的一次演讲，概述了公司的愿景和进展。
 回顾：Mobileye采取三管齐下的策略来开发和部署自动车辆技术，该技术结合了完整的自动驾驶堆栈（包括基于摄像头，雷达和激光雷达技术的冗余传感子系统）及其REM映射系统和规则，责任敏感安全（RSS）驾驶政策。
 Mobileye的REM映射系统实质上是通过利用配备了该技术的近一百万辆汽车来构建数据，以构建可用于支持ADAS和自动驾驶系统的高清地图。沙舒亚说，Mobileye的技术现在可以自动绘制世界地图，每天跟踪近800万公里，迄今为止已完成近10亿公里。
 该公司在CES上提供了更多有关正在开发中的新型激光雷达片上系统产品的详细信息，该产品将于2025年上市。该激光雷达将使用英特尔的专业硅光子学晶圆厂，这一点值得注意，因为Mobileye以基于相机的技术。需要明确的是，Mobileye并没有放弃这种以相机为先的方法。 Shashua解释说Mobileye认为，最好的技术和商业方法是开发摄像头优先系统，并使用激光雷达和雷达作为冗余的附件。
 简而言之：Mobileye拥有足够的资金和现有的网络来将自动车辆技术商业化并推向大众。
             还记得我曾预测自动交付将在2021年获得动力吗？看来有时候我是对的！ 
优步去年以26.5亿美元收购的按需交付创业公司的机器人部门Postmates X正在寻求投资者以争取成为一家名为Serve Robotics的独立公司。
 您可能还记得Serve，这是由Postmates X开发和试行的黄色和黑色装饰的自动人行道送货机器人。该机器人最近与Pink Dot Stores合作，在西好莱坞进行送货，启动。
 我了解了该计划的一些重要细节，但尚未解决。优步将在这家新公司中保持股份。知情人士透露，Uber的股份最初很少，但此后激增至约25％。
 该公司将由阿里·卡沙尼（Ali Kashani）经营，后者领导Postmates X并领导服务计划。 Anthony Armenta将负责该初创公司的软件工作，而Aaron Leiba将负责硬件-保持与Postmates X相同的职位。
